text: Okay. Hello, good evening to everybody. It's a pleasure to be here to... to perform a radical prostatectomy. You know, I don't know if the case has been introduced. We are going to perform a robotic-assisted nerve-sparing prostatectomy, but the patient has three positive biopsies on the right side, at least four plus three. So we would not do a nerve-sparing on the right side, but we do a nerve-sparing on the left side. I think it's a good opportunity to demonstrate the different techniques of wide excision and nerve-sparing in one patient.

Regarding the lymph nodes, here the philosophy is if the Briganti score is not higher than five or six, they don't do the lymph nodes. Briganti score is three in this patient, so they don't do lymph nodes. In my hospital, we would do the lymph nodes, but this is debatable, and I think we should follow the rules of this hospital. Okay, I have to thank the anesthetist who is sitting next to us to do the anesthesia. My colleague is Dr. Dietl, she is assisting.
The surgical team begins port placement for the robotic-assisted prostatectomy, adjusting the patient to the Trendelenburg position to facilitate access to the pelvic region.

We are using an X system, which is very comparable to the SI system. Yeah, there's nothing special about this. Here, they do a docking between the legs. At home, we do a side docking. But it doesn't matter at all where you dock the DaVinci, I think. So I think the side docking is easier. But we should not change a winning team. And they used to do it here, so we should do that.

The surgeon performs a mini laparotomy above the umbilicus, preparing the surgical field. Heparin is visibly administered at the incision site, although the surgeon notes a preference for administration in the arm.

Good afternoon. This is Ignacio Moncada. I'm a moderator in your case with Dr. Scafone. How are you? Good, Mike. Hello. Nice that you're here and I hope we have a good time together. Okay, can you give the port? Camera port, please. So you always do this? This mini laparotomy to go with the truckers or you go just... To tell you the truth, at home we have... We have a Hassan port for all the procedures. So I'm not very familiar.

So can you give me a suture to close this? So we do always mini laparotomy using a Hassan type port as a camera port. Yeah. Because normally I go with the trocar directly without just prick the abdomen and place that. So you can avoid the stitches and there's no leakage of air. Yeah, you're right. I think that you're absolutely right. We always shoot the way that we have learned. I would say the most safest way. For me, this is a very safe way to get access to the optimal cavity. And it works for all ports. So we do like that. Yeah, you're right.

If you want, you can also go directly. But for beginners, I wouldn't recommend. If you start with... laparoscopy of robotic, I would say the mini laparotomy is a very safe access. So bring the da Vinci in. You see it much nicer. So you don't release the additions of the Sigma Now, at the moment, there's no need to do anything here. If we would do lymph nodes, we would extend

This is the disadvantage of the trans access. You have to create the access to the reacher space, but as I said, we only do extra if... The patient doesn't require lymph nodes and the space is a little bit limited compared to the transperitoneal access. And what we did find out is that we have three times more metastasis when we go trans in patients with intermediate risk. So we completely switched in patients where we do lymph nodes to the trans access. But it doesn't take long and it's also a nice exercise for beginners to bring the peritoneum down.
It's a nice, as I said, module one in the modular training to learn radical prostatectomy.

You have a very big experience in laparoscopic radical prostatectomy. What is the role of laparoscopic instead of robotic radical prostatectomy now in your vision? That's a very good question. To answer this, you know, we did in the last three years, we had running the only prospective randomized trial, multicenter, patient-blinded comparing lab prostatectomy versus robotic prostatectomy. And we included seven hundred eighty patients and we finished the recruitment three months ago. And we will come out with the data next year, but I can tell you the results are the first, the primary outcome was the continence, and secondary outcome, oncology, potency, and all this.

There was a clear difference. I can't give you the results now because it's not allowed to do, but I must say it was clearer than I expected. The continence is much better in one group. And we still do lab in my hospital, but this is more due to the fact that we have one robot permanently and we have a second only on two days. So we are fighting for a second all the week. So we have to do lab cases because we have to operate our patients. So this is the vase here, see that? the patient's not so obese and it wouldn't be difficult to perform a lymphadenectomy here. So I don't know if this was a clear answer to your question, but I tried to answer. You didn't say what, I mean, which one was the winner? Yeah, I think it's clear.

But I remember that three or four years ago, we had the same discussion, and the impression was that good laparoscopic surgeon had the same result as with the robotic. Now, it seems to be some differences. But I don't know. I know that in the Martini Clinic, they are doing a very big randomized study on this with not so clear result in the outcome of the different patients. But they compare open versus robotic, don't they? Open laparoscopic and robotic. But I'm a genie, I think they don't do lap. They do mostly open laparoscopic.

The surgeon approaches the endopelvic fascia, identifying small vessels in the area. On the opposite side, similar actions are performed. The surgeon requests an increase in the power of the bipolar cautery due to its insufficient performance. A large vein is noted, which poses a potential risk. The surface of the prostate is being cleaned of fat tissue. The surgeon inquires about the bladder's status and requests it to be emptied.

And that's a nice, you always can use the fourth arm to help yourself. This is endopelvic fascia on the... Can you clean the optic? It's a little bit dirty. Okay, can you clean the optic, please, and empty the bladder? So we could also discuss, you know, the strategy here. So as I said, the patient has a cleason, four plus three carcinoma on the right side and three biopsies out of six on the right side. So... As I said, I would do a wide excision on the right side or partly nerve sparing, but we can do an intrafascial nerve sparing on the left side. In my hospital, we also would do, the camera is not really in, I can't go further in. Can you improve this?

The surgeon begins the prostatectomy with the bladder neck clearly visible. The bladder overlaps the prostate, and the surrounding fat has been removed. The optic is adjusted for a better view of the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the bladder neck by feeling its mobility with an instrument, contrasting it with the solid prostate. Using a ProGrasp, the surgeon pulls slightly to reveal the bladder neck, showing longitudinal muscle fibers running into the prostatic urethra. Blunt dissection is performed to extend the view to the lateral side, ensuring the area is far from the nerves. The bladder neck is preserved, and the dissection is completed around the structure.

The surgeon opens the bladder neck, using cold scissors to avoid energy application that could damage the sphincter. The bladder neck is clearly visible, and the surgeon carefully identifies the correct plane to avoid entering the adenomectomy plane.

Uwe, in the era of MRI, we have the possibility to do MRI before this kind of surgery, and we have MRI in a lot of our patients. You don't consider mandatory to do MRI to do an MRI before this kind of surgery, especially if you want to do a nerve sparing surgery? That's also a very good question. So this patient, he had a PET scan, actually. So showing no metastases and all this. And I think he also had an MRI. And he did not show any... No, it's only a CT scan. A CT scan, yeah. So, yeah, that's... You know, most of our patients, and it is now getting a standard, they do multi-parametric MRIs and fusion biopsies, and then you have an MRI.

So most of the patients. But to tell you the truth, in one third of the patients, we can't agree during the surgery what they described in the MRI. So it's not so precise as I would love to have it. So you don't trust very much if he says there is no capsular disease? It's not the right saying to say I don't trust. I'm critical with the MRI, and I don't believe one hundred percent in the MRI.

The surgeon locates the tips of the seminal vesicles, noting their length, and applies a clip. Electrocautery is used to cut in wide excision patients, while the vas deferens is clipped in nerve-sparing procedures. The optic lens is cleaned to improve visibility, and small vessels are clipped.

The surgeon applies a small metal clip to secure small vessels, using 5mm clips for this purpose. The tips of the seminal vesicle are visible, with the left side clearly identified. The surgeon then moves to the opposite side, pushing down the Denonvilliers' fascia.

And it's always good to... Let me inform you that you're staying extra thirty minutes so we have a connection, a live connection to the parabolic theater until seven thirty. Thank you. Okay, this is... So you have half an hour. Okay, half an hour. It's difficult to make the whole procedure in half an hour, so I try my best. You have forty minutes until seven thirty.

The surgeon identifies two large arteries supplying the seminal vesicles and applies a clip to secure them. The assistant attempts to grasp the tissue on one side, but it remains fixed and requires further manipulation.

The surgeon gains access to the Denonvilliers' fascia, identifying the intrafascial plane and performing a wide excision on the right side. The incision is made directly on the capsule, revealing the pre-rectal fatty tissue. The surgeon proceeds with nerve-sparing techniques, creating a space by opening the Denonvilliers' fascia and moving medially to preserve the pulvostatic ligament, while a vein is opened in the process.

The surgeon clips a vessel, intending to remove the clip during apical dissection. A metal clip is used to prevent migration into the anastomosis, avoiding permanent bleeding. A large vein opening is visible, requiring a stitch with 4-0 Vicryl. The surgeon discusses preserving the pulvostatic ligaments and endopelvic fascia to improve early continence and provide urethral stability.

The surgeon uses a needle holder to place a stitch at the apex to stop bleeding, followed by the use of scissors. Despite increased pressure, there is still some oozing, prompting the surgeon to place another stitch.
The surgeon addresses a bleeding site, successfully stopping the bleeding before proceeding to cut and remove tissue from the surgical field.

The surgeon opens the lateral area further, using suction and energy devices to assist. The denovus fascia is pushed down, and the surgeon stays close to the capsule for interfascial nerve sparing. On this side, a wider excision is performed.

The surgeon adjusts the view using a third-degree optic, cleaning the lens for clarity. The bladder is repositioned downward to improve access to the posterior area. The procedure begins on the side without nerve sparing, using clips and hemologs to create more space and enhance visibility of the surgical field.

The surgeon turns the optic to obtain a clearer view of the prostate apex. The surgical plane is visible, and the surgeon begins mobilizing the tissue, staying very close to the anatomical plane. The surrounding structures, including the winds, are now more clearly defined.

The surgeon is performing an intrafacial dissection, focusing on nerve preservation on the right side. The surgical field shows clear orientation of the prostate capsule, with visible placement of a clip for hemostasis.

The surgeon creates space near the apex and places a clip on a small vessel. As the dissection progresses closer to the apex, structures are peeled off without the need for additional clips. The interfacial nerve-sparing technique is employed, with nerves covered and not visible. The optic is adjusted to aid in finding the dissection plane, which is visible ventrally and laterally.

The surgeon identifies the ligament medially and uses energy to manage the area. A large vein is visible, similar to the opposite side, and the tissue is completely peeled off. The surgeon requests a stitch to control minor venous bleeding, which is not severe.

The surgeon carefully controls bleeding during the dissection, ensuring a clear laparoscopic view. The neurovascular bundle and nerves are covered by a thin periprostatic fascia, which is no longer on the prostate surface but planar over the structures. Veins and arteries are visible beneath the fascia.

The surgeon is working within the endoperic fissure, requesting scissors to cut through the tissue. A clip is placed on the pedicle near the prostate, but the presence of adhesions requires careful manipulation. The optic is turned to enhance visibility of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to cut tissue in the interfacial plane, moving more laterally on the right side due to the presence of a Gleason dominant fork. Emolox clips are applied to secure structures, with a focus on minimizing trauma compared to electrocautery. The nerves are partially preserved, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon cleans the lens to improve visibility, revealing the nearly completely mobilized prostate with a clear distinction between bulky structures and remaining small nerves. The optic is turned to free the apex, exposing the urethra, which is now completely visible.

The surgeon adjusts the camera to a thirty-degree downward angle and begins the apical dissection of the prostate. There is minor bleeding observed, and the surgeon follows the anatomical contours of the prostate, attempting to mobilize the lateral tissues as much as possible.

The surgeon identifies a superficial vein and requests cleaning of the optic lens. A needle holder is used to stitch the vein, preferring this method over cutting. The surgeon stitches under the ligaments and repeats the process on the opposite side.

The surgeon stitches the wings, ensuring the ligaments remain superficial to the stitch. The prostate is mobilized, and the apex is carefully developed step by step. Suction and scissors are used to clear the area, with caution advised to avoid excessive energy use at the apex to prevent damage to the sphincter fibers and nerves.

The surgeon performs apical dissection, carefully navigating the high-risk area near the apex to avoid cutting the sphincter and risking incontinence. The end of the prostate is visible, and the surgeon identifies the plane for dissection, ensuring not to leave parts of the prostate inside. The procedure involves suturing the urethra to the bladder and covering it with surrounding structures.

The surgeon performs the same dissection on the opposite side, applying tension to the prostate while managing open veins. A running suture is planned to stop bleeding after completing the cutting, using a two-layer Rocco stitch followed by a Frio strata fix for the anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies open veins near the prostate, which are not ligated, and discusses the importance of careful stitching to avoid damaging the sphincter. The apical dissection involves cutting the junction between the striated sphincter and prostate, revealing the Sundarini fascia and the inner layer of the urethra with visible longitudinal muscle fibers. The surgeon aims to create a longer urethral stump by dissecting further, while confirming the presence of the catheter.

The surgeon identifies the varomentanum deep inside the small prostate, noting its inclusion in the specimen. The dissection proceeds distally to the varomentanum, ensuring careful separation to avoid cutting into the neurovascular bundle. The prostate is visible running along the surgical field.
